coredata.dc:title: Development of turn-on fluorescent sensor based on green emissive carnosine-modified tannic acid carbon dots for the selective detection of ascorbic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Sangubotla, Roopkumar
Kim, Jongsung
coredata.dc:description: Ascorbic acid (AA) is an indispensable biological antioxidant with anti-aging potential in neurological disorders such as Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s. Its abnormal concentrations in physiological fluids like human serum can lead to neurological complications. In this study, a turn-on fluorescent sensor was developed using dipeptide (i.e., carnosine) modification of the polyphenolic tannic acid (TA) carbon dots (CDs) (TATA-CDs) for AA detection. Blue-fluorescent TATA-CDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal treatment of the TA and ethylene glycol-bis(β-aminoethyl ether)-N,N,N′,N′-tetraacetic acid (EGTA) as a nitrogen (N) dopant. TATA-CDs were functionalized with carnosine (TATA-Car CDs) using a facile stirring reaction. Their morphological and optical properties were studied via TEM, XRD, UV–Vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy. TATA-Car CDs displayed green fluorescence with excitation and emission wavelength maxima of 400 and 509 nm, respectively. These novel CDs are responsible for the selective turn-on fluorescence sensing of AA (0 to 15 nM) when mixed with copper (Cu2+) ions and deliver a low detection limit of 1.34 nM. The practicality and feasibility of TATA-Car CDs were investigated in human serum samples. From this perspective, the proposed fluorescent sensor is more suitable for the real-time monitoring of AA in different neurological disorders.

head.ce:title: Development of turn-on fluorescent sensor based on green emissive carnosine-modified tannic acid carbon dots for the selective detection of ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam Daero, Seongnam-Si, Gyeonggi-Do 13120, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam Daero, Seongnam-Si, Gyeonggi-Do 13120, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ascorbic acid (AA) is an indispensable biological antioxidant with anti-aging potential in neurological disorders such as Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s. Its abnormal concentrations in physiological fluids like human serum can lead to neurological complications. In this study, a turn-on fluorescent sensor was developed using dipeptide (i.e., carnosine) modification of the polyphenolic tannic acid (TA) carbon dots (CDs) (TATA-CDs) for AA detection. Blue-fluorescent TATA-CDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal treatment of the TA and ethylene glycol-bis(β-aminoethyl ether)-N,N,N′,N′-tetraacetic acid (EGTA) as a nitrogen (N) dopant. TATA-CDs were functionalized with carnosine (TATA-Car CDs) using a facile stirring reaction. Their morphological and optical properties were studied via TEM, XRD, UV–Vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy. TATA-Car CDs displayed green fluorescence with excitation and emission wavelength maxima of 400 and 509 nm, respectively. These novel CDs are responsible for the selective turn-on fluorescence sensing of AA (0 to 15 nM) when mixed with copper (Cu2+) ions and deliver a low detection limit of 1.34 nM. The practicality and feasibility of TATA-Car CDs were investigated in human serum samples. From this perspective, the proposed fluorescent sensor is more suitable for the real-time monitoring of AA in different neurological disorders.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA) is a potent antioxidant and biomarker that can prevent free radical formation in cells and treat Parkinson’s disease (PD) patients [1–3]. AA also has curable properties against tumors, diabetes, scurvy, cardiomyopathy, and aging-related problems, and in vitamin form (vitamin C), it can be thoroughly obtained from the human diet [4]. Excessive AA consumption in the human body can develop simple symptoms (i.e., headache, vomiting, and diarrhea) or moderate complications (i.e., insomnia and renal disturbance) [5]. The interference due to the coexistence of ions, biomolecules, and neurotransmitters is highly hindering the AA sensing in complex biological fluids [6]. Thus, highly specific analytical sensors need to be developed for complex samples like human serum for the convenient detection of AA, which facilitates the diagnosis of scurvy. Currently, the traditional methods for AA sensing, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), electrochemistry, and capillary electrophoresis, are highly expensive and require sophisticated personnel [7–9]. Recently, advanced integrity among electrochemistry, fluorescence, and molecular imprinting technology (MIT) has been demanding research interest in the sensing field [10,11]. Alberti et al., developed an electrochemical sensor using molecularly imprinted electrosynthesized polymer (e-MIP) over screen printed electrodes, which was supplemented with polypyrrole overoxidation to enable AA sensing [12]. Though the sensor is cost-effective and works in a wide linear range of AA, its sensitivity needs to be enhanced. In another study, researchers developed potentially selective MIPs and MXene electrochemical sensors for AA sensing [13]. The design of the sensor requires sophisticated personnel to rule out the problems encountered during the sensing assay. Pirot et al., developed a fluorescence-based sensor using MIPs and zeolitic imidazole framework-8 (ZIF-8) to facilitate significant molecular recognition as well as increased surface area and chemical stability, respectively. Further, fluorescence response was obtained through the functionalization of ZIF-8 by copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) and carbon dots (CDs) as the signal transducers, respectively [14]. Despite its suitable linear range and low sensitivity, the sensor can still be optimized at nanomolar AA levels in the future. Recently, researchers have developed colorimetric sensors that are mediated through metal oxide-based nanosheets and microspheres for AA sensing [15,16]. The other metal–organic framework (MOF)-based sensors were developed for the detection of AA [17,18]. However, the above-mentioned studies require further investigation at the in vitro level to open new windows. Also, a few methods are complex in terms of design, methodology, and cost burden.

In this regard, fluorescence-based methods have gained researchers attention owing to their beneficial features such as their facile synthesis approach, biocompatibility, cost-effectiveness, and excellent sensing performance. Previously, many methods based on the blue-fluorescence emission and turn-off fluorescence response were familiar. Rao et al., developed TA-based copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) for AA sensing through the mediation of iron (Fe3+) ions [19]. In another study, nanohybrids based on cadmium telluride (CdTe) QDs linked with silica NPs were probed for AA sensing [20]. Unfortunately, the reported sensors are restricted by low sensitivity and the use of toxic metallic QDs. Chen et al., designed a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based sensor for AA sensing using CdTe QDs and gold NPs (Au NPs) as both fluorophores and quenchers, respectively [21]. Despite its adequate sensitivity and broad linear range, the sensor has suffered from its complex methodology. Nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) prepared from malic acid and urea showed concentration-dependent fluorescence and were utilized for the detection of AA. Unfortunately, micromolar sensitivity and turn-off fluorescence response seem to be the limiting features of this sensor [22].

The CDs are particularly ultra-small carbon materials with particle sizes of 10 nm or even less, which signify sensing, bio-imaging, bio-labeling, and other biomedical fields [23–28]. Their facile synthesis, tremendous biocompatibility, and attractive electronic and optical properties are highly advantageous in contrast to the previous semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [29]. The CDs are extremely convenient nanomaterials that can facilitate the thorough modification of different functional ligands, proteins, and biomolecules [30–32]. So far, surface-modified CDs have been adopted for the detection of metal ions and cellular pH and temperature [33–35].

Tannic acid (TA), a natural polyphenol mostly comprised of multiple hydroxyl (–OH) moieties, can stop free radicals from doing damage and fight the aging process by acting as an “antioxidant” [36]. As it consists of hydrophilic moieties, it can act as both a reducing and stabilizing agent with excellent chelation properties. For instance, Cao et al., made silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) with a single size distribution by using different amounts of TA and Ag2+ ions [37]. TA is specifically applied as a biocompatible precursor for preparing highly sensitive fluorescent CDs for the detection of metal ions [38,39].

Carnosine (Car), a dipeptide β-alanyl-L-histidine available in high amounts in the brain and heart muscles. Its potential antioxidant features can aid in wound healing by inhibiting cell damage. Also, its protective roles against skin aging were proven through its reduced lipid peroxidation properties [40]. In addition, carnosine has been utilized to inhibit other inflammations and gastric ulcers [41]. The proposed work was inspired by the superior chelating properties of carnosine with Cu2+ and further mediation of the fluorescence turn-on behavior with AA, which together encouraged the highly sensitive and selective detection of AA.

For the last two decades, numerous fluorescence-based sensors have been developed for AA sensing. Wang and his colleagues used semiconductor QDs (cadmium selenide and telluride, or CdSe and CdTe) and silica nanoparticles (SiO2 NPs), to design hybrid nanostructures [20]. Though the sensor showed adequate sensitivity with nanomolar detection (35 nM) of AA, unfortunately, its hazardous chemical nature exerts a toxic environment for the further development of the sensor. In another study, authors synthesized nitrogen-doped CDs using two simple precursors such as malic acid and urea from microwave irradiation that exhibited AA sensing in a broader linear range (0–60 μM) [22]. But the low sensitivity (2.6 μM) and turn-off fluorescence response are the major limitations of the sensor. Ma and his co-authors designed a fluorescence turn-off–on approach using Cu2+ ions as a mediator for AA sensing [42]. The usage of large quantities of strong acids such as concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4) and nitric acid (HNO3) during the preparation of CDs is strongly limiting their further application in biological studies. Moreover, its limited sensitivity (60 μM) for AA detection is also inadequate for the future utility of the sensor. Despite highly sensitive methods being available, a few kinds of fluorescent sensors have been dealing with minimal sensitivity, a high cost burden, moderate selectivity, and a low signal-to-noise ratio for their detection. To the best of our knowledge, the proposed work is beneficial because it comprises turn-on sensors, non-toxic precursors, a facile synthesis approach, desirable sensitivity, and high selectivity.

In this work, we synthesized TATA-CDs by adapting the hydrothermal method with a low carbonization degree. Also, these TATA-CDs were modified with a dipeptide ligand called carnosine to make them more hydrophilic and fluorescent. With the mediation of Cu2+ ions, the green-emitting TATA-Car CDs that were made gave excellent sensitivity for AA sensing. The practicality of TATA-Car CDs was studied in human serum samples, which recommended the feasibility of the sensor for AA determination. Noteworthy, turn-on fluorescence-based sensors are adequately convenient and can provide an enhanced signal-to-noise ratio during fluorometric measurements as compared to turn-off sensors. The sensitive detection of AA was achieved due to the rich hydroxyl (OH) and nitrogen (C-N) contents of the TATA-Car CDs.

Tannic acid (TA), ethylene glycol-bis(β-aminoethyl ether)-N,N,N′,N′-tetraacetic acid (EGTA), carnosine, L-ascorbic acid (AA), copper sulphate (CuSO4), glucose (Glu), dopamine (DA), epinephrine (EP), L-glutathione (GSH), serotonin (5-HT), L-cysteine (Cys), aluminum chloride (AlCl3), magnesium hydroxide (Mg(OH)2), calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)2), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), Tin(II) chloride (SnCl2), cobalt (II) sulfate (CoSO4), manganese (II) acetate (C4H6MnO4), cadmium nitrate (Cd(NO3)2), nickel (II) hydroxide (Ni(OH)2), lead (II) nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), barium acetate (Ba(C2H3O2)2, iron (II) sulfate (FeSO4), zinc nitrate (Zn(NO3)2), potassium bromide (KBr), potassium iodide (KI), potassium chlorate (KClO3), potassium nitrate (KNO3), sodium dichromate (Na2Cr2O7), and potassium permanganate (KMnO4) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich. Deionized (DI) water and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 10 mM, pH 8.0) were used for the fluorescence measurements.

A Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN 200 kV electron microscope was employed for the measurements of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images. A Bruker Vertex high-resolution 70 FT-IR spectrometer was employed for recording Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. Thermo Scientific K-AlphaTM X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer with a range of motion (100–4000 eV), a 128-channel detector, and a double-focusing hemispherical analyzer (180°) with a micro-focused X-ray source (Al Kα) was used to measure the XPS spectra. Rigaku Ultima III X-ray diffractometer, equipped with a Cu sealed tube of λ = 1.54,178 Å), 40 kV, 30 mA, and a scan speed of 2°/min in continuous scanning 2θ mode, was used for X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. A Varian Cary 100 UV–Vis spectrophotometer was utilized for measuring UV–Vis absorption spectra. Fluorescence measurements were performed by a Horiba Nanolog spectrofluorometer with a 450-W Xenon lamp and an excitation source (a Xenon arc lamp, power > 400 W) connected with high-speed USB. The PTI EasyLife II fluorometer system with auto-adjustable excitation wavelengths (260–650 nm) was used to acquire the fluorescence lifetime decay data. EasyLife II software with RS232 and the USB connection was used for curve fitting the data. Thermal stability studies were investigated using the SDT Q600 (V20.9 Build 20) (TGA-DTA) analyzer under N2 (heating rate: 10 °C min−1, flow rate: 100 mL min−1. A Thermo Scientific pH meter was employed for pH calibration.

TATA-CDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal treatment of TA and EGTA. Before beginning the experiment, all glassware was cleansed using aqua regia (HCl:HNO3 = 3:1, v/v). TA (2 g) and EGTA (1 g) were dissolved in a beaker containing DI water (50 mL) and continuously stirred for 1 h until a homogenous solution was formed. The reaction mixture was sealed in a 75-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h in an oven. A dark-colored solution was formed, which was eventually washed several times with the ethanol and water mixture to remove impurities and residual TA and EGTA moieties. Further, the solution was centrifuged (9,000 rpm for 20 min) and dialyzed against DI water using a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 100–500 D) for 24 h to remove further residual components. The purified TATA-CDs were dried in an oven for 24 h to obtain the powder and refrigerated for further functionalization procedures. The powdered TATA-CDs appeared in a dark-black color, as shown in Fig. S1A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and purification of TATA-Car CDs
ce:section.ce:para: TATA-Car CDs were prepared using previously synthesized TATA-CDs. In this reaction, TATA-CDs (0.075 g) were mixed with carnosine (0.25 g) in a beaker containing tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 8) and gently stirred for 3 h at 80 °C. Following this, a creamy solution was obtained, and the solution was thoroughly rinsed with water and centrifuged (9000 rpm, 20 min). The resulting TATA-Car CDs solution was dialyzed for 24 h through a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 100–500 D), and TATA-Car CDs were dried in an oven and refrigerated for further studies. Fig. S1B shows the powdered TATA-Car CDs, which appeared in a dark-brown color. Scheme 1 illustrates the synthesis and functionalization steps.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA by TATA-Car CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 300 μL of TATA-Car CDs (1.0 mg mL−1), 500 μL of PBS (10 mM; pH 8), and 500 μL of CuSO4 (1 μM) were mixed into the cuvette containing 2 mL of DI water. Different concentrations of AA (0–15 nM) were added to this cuvette and kept for incubation for 30 min, and the fluorescence spectra were measured using an excitation wavelength of 400 nm.

The practicality of the TATA-Car CDs was investigated in real biological samples, like human serum, for the detection of AA. The serum samples were centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 20 min, and then 0.5 mL of acetonitrilesolution was added to the supernatant samples to precipitate the proteins in the serum. Further, the supernatant solutions were again centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 20 min. The obtained samples were 50-fold diluted by employing PBS (10 mM, pH 8.0). A series of spiked samples were prepared by adding different concentrations of AA (2.5, 5, 7.5, and 15 nM). These spiked samples were finally used for the detection of AA via the proposed TATA-Car CDs.

The primary precursor used for this study was tannic acid due to its non-toxic nature, hydrophilic properties, cost-effectiveness, and presence of a conjugated aromatic structure including numerous hydroxyl (OH) groups. EGTA was chosen as a co-reactant due to its capability to introduce heteroatoms by doping. The utilization of carnosine as a functional ligand has been chosen to enhance the hydrophilicity, fluorescence tunability, and chelating capabilities of Cu2+ ions.

The TEM images of TATA-CDs show spherical and uniformly dispersed particles ( Fig. S2A and B). No lattice fringes were observed from the HRTEM images of TATA-CDs, which represented their amorphous nature ( Fig. S2C). These results imply that the presence of pure TA moieties in the TATA-CDs may have contributed to their amorphous characteristics. The inset image shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern, which is consistent with the HRTEM image of TATA-CDs, revealing their amorphous nature. The particle size distribution curve was drawn based on the TEM images, which showed the average particle size to be around 9 nm ( Fig. S2D). These results are corroborated by the TEM images of earlier reported TA-based CDs [43]. The TEM images of TATA-Car CDs revealed that the particles are spherical, as shown in Fig. 1 A and B. The HRTEM image of TATA-Car CDs has revealed no lattice fringes, which confirms the amorphous nature of the particles ( Fig. 1C). The inset image from Fig. 1C indicates the SAED pattern and confirms its amorphous nature. Further, TEM images of TATA-Car CDs revealed a clear spherical morphology with increased particle sizes around 12 nm ( Fig. 1D). Based on these results, we suggest that the modification of carnosine (i.e., TATA-Car CDs) may have changed the size and shape of the as-synthesized TATA-CDs by changing its surface chemistry or adding more functional groups to its surface.

The powder XRD patterns for the TA, TATA-CDs, and TATA-Car CDs were analyzed ( Fig. 2 A). The XRD pattern of the pure TA has exhibited a broader peak at 2θ = 25.2°, representing the amorphous nature of TA without noticing any peaks of crystalline phases (curve i) [44]. Also, the XRD pattern of the TATA-CDs has revealed a similar centrally positioned peak (2θ = 22.7°), indicating the amorphous phase of TA (curve ii). These results corroborate the previous tannic acid-derived CDs [45,46]. It is signifying that the XRD pattern of TATA-Car CDs has shown a broader peak at (2θ = 21.6°) without any further peaks of crystalline phases and confirming its highly amorphous nature (curve iii). The thermal stability of TATA-CDs and TATA-Car CDs was investigated using TGA analysis in the temperature range of 0–700 °C, as shown in Fig. 2 B. The TGA plot of TATA-CDs showed that they lost 70.2% of their weight due to the loss of water and other functional groups like carbonyl (C = O) and amine (N-H)/(C-N), etc. Next, the TGA plot of TATA-Car CDs showed a weight loss of 61.9%, which results from the decomposition of the above-mentioned moieties. It is suggested that TATA-Car CDs are more thermally stable than TATA-CDs owing to the surface modification of carnosine.

The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) analyses were conducted to examine the pristine tannic acid, TATA-CDs, and TATA-Car CDs in order to get insights into the functionalization process. The FT-IR spectra of tannic acid show the typical peaks for the stretching vibrations of phenol groups at 3122 cm−1 (aromatic, O-H), 1181 cm−1 (C-O), 1397 (aromatic, C-O-C), 1536 cm−1 and 1615 cm−1 (C = C), and the ester functional groups at around 1712 cm−1 (C = O) (line i, Fig. 2 C) [47]. The FT-IR spectra of TATA-CDs have also revealed the peaks of similar phenolic stretching vibrations with a slight shift, including 3149 (aromatic, O-H), 1043 cm−1 (C-O), 1397 (aromatic, C-O-C), 1531 cm−1 and 1623 cm−1 (C = C), and 1718 cm−1 (C = O), as shown in line ii, Fig. 2C. The peaks around 1248 and 1482 cm−1 from the FT-IR spectra of TATA-CDs are responsible for the stretching vibrations of (C-N) groups, which suggests that the carbonization of EGTA moieties with TA groups occurred during the formation of TATA-CDs ( Fig. 2C, line ii) [48,49]. Further, the FT-IR spectra of functionalized TATA-Car CDs have shown similar slightly shifted phenolic stretching vibrations around 3114 (aromatic, O-H), 1154 cm−1 (C-O), 1401 (aromatic, C-O-C), 1614 cm−1 (C = C), and 1724 cm−1 (C = O) (line iii, Fig. 2C). The peak at 1242 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of C-N moieties. The multiple peaks at 3439 and 3515 cm−1 (from the FT-IR spectra of TATA-CDs) and the peaks at 3410, 3478, and 3545 cm−1 (from the FT-IR spectra of TATA-Car CDs) indicate the (N-H) functional groups. The presence of amide bonds in the FT-IR spectra of TATA-Car CDs suggests that the TATA-CDs were successfully modified by the carnosine ligand via covalent bonding.

The elemental constitution and chemical species of the TATA-CDs and TATA-Car CDs were investigated through XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. S3A, the total spectrum of TATA-CDs showed chemical species with binding energies of 284.88, 532.15, and 401.83 eV for C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s, respectively. The high-resolution individual XPS spectra for the synthesized TATA-CDs are shown in Fig. S3B-D. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s show four deconvoluted peaks at 284.79, 286.04, 286.95, and 288.99 eV, which are responsible for the C-C/C = C, C-O/C-N, C = O, and O-C = O, respectively ( Fig. S3B). The XPS spectra of the O 1 s exhibit distinct peaks corresponding to the C-OH/C-O-C and O-C = O functional groups at about 532.32 and 533.73 eV, respectively ( Fig. S3C). The XPS spectrum of N 1 s revealed a central peak at 402.36 eV, which was assigned to the N-H group, as shown in Fig. S3D.

Also, the XPS spectra of the TATA-Car CDs show that they have a similar chemical environment. This suggests that the ligand carnosine has functionalized with the prepared TATA-CDs. Fig. 3 A shows the total survey spectrum of TATA-Car CDs, wherein the chemical species demonstrate binding energies of 284.37, 530.46, 399.45, and 196.95 eV for C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s, and Cl 2p, respectively. Fig. 3B-E has revealed the individual high-resolution XPS spectra of TATA-Car CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s show deconvoluted peaks at 284.884, 286.04, 286.95, and 288.99 eV, which correspond to the C-C/C = C, C-O/C-N, C = O, and O-C = O, respectively ( Fig. 3B). Fig. 3C shows the C-O, C-OH/C-O-C, and O-C = O groups around 529.87, 532.32, and 533.73 eV. The XPS spectrum of the N 1 s revealed the presence of two distinct peaks at about 398.68 and 400.16 eV for the C-N and N-H functional groups ( Fig. 3D). The presence of Cl 2p species at 197.76 eV in the XPS spectrum of TATA-Car CDs has been detected, which may be attributed to the tris-HCl buffer, as shown in Fig. 3E. Based on the results, it can be assumed that the interaction between TATA-CDs and carnosine has led to the formation of amide bonds through covalent bonding. XPS results also corroborate the FT-IR results of TATA-Car CDs.

The optical properties of the TATA-CDs and TATA-Car CDs were investigated in DI water by employing UV–Vis absorbance spectroscopy. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the TATA-CDs (red curve) and TATA-Car CDs (blue curve) are shown in Fig. 4 A. The UV–Vis spectrum of TATA-CDs exhibited two absorption bands at 205 and 267 nm, which were attributed to the π -π* transition of the aromatic sp2 carbon (C = C) domains and the n-π* transition of the surface functional groups (C = O/C-N), respectively (red curve, Fig. 4A). The latter absorption band at 267 nm was responsible for the absorption of the EGTA precursor. Further, the UV–Vis spectrum of TATA-Car CDs displayed two similar absorption bands around 223 and 259 nm, which correspond to the π -π* and n-π* transitions, respectively. In addition, a slight shift in the absorption bands and the raised absorption portion in the TATA-Car CDs collectively confirm the sufficient modification of TATA-CDs with the carnosine ligand. The PL emission maximum of TATA-CDs has been observed at an emission wavelength of 423 nm under an excitation wavelength of 340 nm ( Fig. 4B). The inset image represents the blue emission of TATA-CDs under the UV–Vis illuminator. Fig. 4C shows the excitation-dependent emission property of TATA-CDs from 340 nm to the 420 nm excitation wavelength with an interval of 10 nm. Fig. 5 A shows the PL emission spectrum of TATA-Car CDs in DI water, which showed an emission maximum of 509 nm under the excitation wavelength of 400 nm. Fig. 5B shows the excitation-dependent emission property of TATA-Car CDs in the excitation wavelength range of 350–430 nm. The excitation and emission contour map reveals the respective excitationand emission wavelengths of TATA-Car CDs, as shown in Fig. 5C. The three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence emission spectra of the TATA-Car CDs with corresponding excitationwavelengths changing from 350 to 430 nm in 10 nm increments are shown in Fig. 5D [50]. According to existing research, it has been observed that CDs derived alone from the TA or in combination with other ligands exhibit emission behavior that is not dependent on excitation. This behavior is mostly attributed to the emission of surface molecular states [36,39,51,52]. In this study, however, both TATA-CDs and TATA-Car CDs exhibited the excitation-dependent emission property, which is attributed to the presence of a diversity of surface functional groups, including amine, carboxyl, and hydroxyl moieties [53]. In addition, we believe that the incorporation of an EGTA ligand has an effect on this property, and these results appear to be too similar to those previously reported for tannic acid-functionalized graphitic carbon nitride CQDs [43]. The quantum yields of TATA-CDs and TATA-Car CDs were found to be 5.6% and 4.8%, respectively.

The sensing performance of the TATA-Car CDs for AA sensing was studied by measuring PL spectroscopy. We measured the fluorescence using TATA-Car CDs treated with Cu2+ (1 µM) as a fluorescence sensor to detect AA ( Fig. 6 A). Fluorescence enhancement of TATA-Car CDs-Cu2+ was examined in various concentrations of AA (0–15 nM) (from bottom to top: 0, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, and 15 nM). Fig. 6B shows the linear regression curve, which was plotted between [(F − F0)/F0] and AA concentrations (0–15 nM), with a good correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9955. F0 and F indicate the fluorescence intensity of TATA-Car CDs-Cu2+ before and after the treatment of AA. Good sensitivity was delivered by the TATA-Car CDs in the linear concentration range of 0–15 nM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 1.34 nM. The LOD was calculated using the 3σ/k equation, where σ and k denote the standard deviation and the slope of the regression curve [54]. The selectivity of the TATA-Car CDs was investigated by measuring the fluorescence spectra against AA using various interferants ( Fig. 6C). Different monovalent and divalent metal ions were used as interferants against Cu2+ for measuring the PL spectra of TATA-Car CDs. To investigate the interference of TATA-Car CDs, high concentrations (5 µM) of interferants compared to Cu2+ (1 µM) were used. A substantial decrease in fluorescence intensity was found in the TATA-Car CDs upon treatment with Cu2+ ions. In contrast, the TATA-Car CDs treated with other interferants failed to demonstrate noticeable quenching. Moreover, the selectivity of the TATA-Car CDs-Cu2+ for AA sensing was examined by fluorescence measurements of various interferants such as Glu, DA, EP, GSH, 5-HT, Cys, SO4-, Al3+, and Cl- ( Fig. 6D). For this study, the concentrations of the aforementioned interferants were set at 50 nM, while the concentration of AA was maintained at 15 nM. The TATA-Car CDs-Cu2+ exhibited fluorescence enhancement when interacting with AA, in comparison to the fluorescence behavior seen with interferants. Fig. 6E shows the corresponding fluorescence emission peaks of TATA-Car CDs treated with Cu2+ (1 µM) and the same with the addition of AA (15 nM). The maximum emission wavelength of 512 nm was observed due to the addition of PBS buffer to the TATA-Car CDs treated with Cu2+. The top image in the inset is associated with the green color emission of TATA-Car CDs-Cu2+ after adding AA (15 nM). In contrast, the bottom image depicts the quenching of PL in TATA-Car CDs-Cu2+ when subjected to UV–Vis irradiation.

In order to validate the PL mechanism of the proposed sensor, we performed the fluorescence lifetime decay analysis, as depicted in Fig. S5 . The fitting of the decay curves was based on the double exponential model, which is shown in the given equation:
(1)

Here, D, τ1 and τ2, and A1 and A2 are the PL decay, lifetime, and pre-exponential factors, respectively. The lifetimes τ1 and τ2 were observed around 8.26 ns and 0.019 ns, respectively, for the TATA-Car CDs-Cu2+. After the AA treatment, lifetimes τ1 and τ2 of TATA-Car CDs-Cu2+ were increased to 13.19 ns and 0.024 ns, respectively. These results suggest that the restoration of fluorescence occurred in the TATA-Car CDs-Cu2+ due to the addition of AA. Hence, the proposed TATA-Car CDs act as suitable fluorescent sensors for AA detection in the nanomolar range through the mediation of Cu2+ ions.

The fluorescence emission of the TATA-Car CDs was studied under different pH conditions, ionic solutions, and time intervals. The TATA-Car CDs showed a maximum fluorescence intensity at pH 8, which is due to the hydrolyzable tannic acid moieties at this pH ( Fig. S6A). But, under acidic and alkaline conditions, changes in the PL intensities were examined, which is due to the availability of multiple (–OH) groups. Fig. S6B shows the fluorescence intensity of the TATA-Car CDs at various NaCl molar concentrations. The results suggest that the ionic stability of the TATA-Car CDs is satisfactory. The fluorescence intensity of the TATA-Car CDs has not altered within the 30-day period, confirming the effectiveness of the sensor ( Fig. S6C). After being treated with AA (15 nM) for a month, the fluorescence behavior of the(i) TATA-Car CDs, (ii) TATA-Car CDs-Cu2+, and TATA-Car CDs-Cu2+ with AA was observed, as shown in Fig. S6D. It is suggested that the fluorescence intensities of the TATA-Car CDs, TATA-Car CDs-Cu2+, and TATA-Car CDs-Cu2+ with AA were not altered even after storing for 30 days. From these observations, it is suggested that the proposed TATA-Car CDs are highly sensitive against AA detection for a prolonged period of time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of the TATA-Car CDs was studied for the detection of AA in real biological samples, i.e., human serum. The spiked samples were prepared using human serum at different concentrations of AA (2.5, 5, 7.5, and 15 nM). Table 2 shows the recovery percentages (95.9–106.3%) for human serum. The relative standard deviation (RSD) values for the human serum were found to be 1.26–5.98%. These results suggest the validation of the developed sensor for AA sensing in real biological samples.

In this study, we developed a turn-on sensor using green emissive TATA-Car CDs. The TATA-Car CDs are highly sensitive for AA detection through the interaction of Cu2+ ions. When TATA-Car CDs were added with copper (Cu2+) ions, it was possible to selectively turn on the fluorescence of AA (0 to 15 nM) and obtain a low detection limit of 1.34 nM. Human serum was utilized as a real biological sample for detecting AA using the proposed sensor. The turn-on strategy for the AA determination using the TATA-Car CDs was allowed to reduce the noise issues while measuring the fluorescence spectra. The morphological and optical characterizations of the prepared TATA-CDs and TATA-Car CDs were studied using different analytical tools. The chemical, ionic, and pH stabilities of the proposed sensor are highly convenient. In the future, this sensor can be studied in vitro and in animal models for a further understanding of its sensing and optical properties.
